Together, the Shape abstract class and Rectangle concrete implementation provide a robust and extensible system for managing geometric entities and their spatial relationships within dnd-kit. These components are critical for algorithms such as those described in Collision Detection Algorithms and other utilities that rely on precise spatial understanding. The module packages/geometry/src/shapes/index.ts serves as an aggregation point, re-exporting these core geometric definitions for simplified access.

Distance and Threshold Utilities
ts
// Assuming Coordinates and other types are defined as in the source.
// import type {Coordinates} from '../types';
// import type {Distance} from './types';

function exceedsDistance(
  { x, y }: Coordinates,
  distance: Distance
): boolean {
  const dx = Math.abs(x);
  const dy = Math.abs(y);

  if (typeof distance === 'number') {
    return Math.sqrt(dx ** 2 + dy ** 2) > distance; // Numeric threshold
  }

  if ('x' in distance && 'y' in distance) {
    return dx > distance.x && dy > distance.y; // Full 2D threshold
  }

  if ('x' in distance) {
    return dx > distance.x; // Horizontal threshold
  }

  if ('y' in distance) {
    return dy > distance.y; // Vertical threshold
  }

  return false;
}

// Example Usages:
const currentPosition = { x: 10, y: 15 };

// Numeric threshold
exceedsDistance(currentPosition, 5); // false, sqrt(10^2 + 15^2) is ~18 > 5

// Full 2D Coordinates as threshold
exceedsDistance(currentPosition, { x: 8, y: 12 }); // true, 10 > 8 && 15 > 12

// Partial Coordinates (only 'x') as threshold
exceedsDistance(currentPosition, { x: 12 }); // false, 10 is not > 12

// Partial Coordinates (only 'y') as threshold
exceedsDistance(currentPosition, { y: 10 }); // true, 15 > 10
The dnd-kit geometry package provides utilities for performing distance and threshold calculations, which are crucial for various aspects of drag-and-drop interactions, such as collision detection and activation constraints. The core of this functionality lies in the exceedsDistance function and the polymorphic Distance type, defined within the packages/geometry/src/distance directory.

The exceedsDistance function (packages/geometry/src/distance/distance.ts) determines if a set of relative coordinates (e.g., a draggable element's movement delta) has surpassed a specified threshold. This function is designed for flexibility, accommodating different ways to define "distance." For example, it can compare the Euclidean distance of a 2D movement against a single numeric threshold. Alternatively, it can evaluate if the movement along the X-axis exceeds a specific horizontal threshold and, independently, if the movement along the Y-axis exceeds a vertical threshold. It also supports checking only the X-axis or only the Y-axis against a given value.

This flexibility is enabled by the Distance type (packages/geometry/src/distance/types.ts), which is a union type capable of representing a distance as a simple number, a full 2D Coordinates object (with x and y components), or a partial Coordinates object specifying only an x or y component. This allows developers to precisely define the conditions under which a drag operation should activate or a collision should be considered. These geometric primitives, including Coordinates, are discussed further in Geometric Primitives: Points and Coordinates.

Reactive State Management
dnd-kit utilizes an internal reactive state management system built upon Preact Signals. This system is primarily designed for internal use by the toolkit's core components and framework adapters, rather than for direct external consumption by developers. It provides mechanisms for managing reactive values, optimizing updates, tracking state history, and integrating reactivity into class-based structures.

Central to this system are primitives for handling reactive state, re-exported from @preact/signals-core via packages/state/src/index.ts. These include signal for creating reactive values, batch for grouping updates to prevent unnecessary re-renders, effect for managing side effects, and untracked for executing code without creating reactive dependencies. This allows for fine-grained control over when and how parts of the application react to state changes.

For derived reactive values, a custom computed function, defined in packages/state/src/computed.ts, enhances the standard signal behavior. It enables the use of an optional comparator function, often deepEqual from packages/state/src/comparators.ts, to prevent updates if the newly computed value is functionally identical to the previous one, even if its reference has changed. This optimization is crucial for performance by avoiding redundant re-evaluations and updates.

The toolkit also provides decorators for integrating reactivity directly into class properties and methods, as seen in packages/state/src/decorators.ts. The reactive decorator transforms a class accessor into a signal, ensuring automatic updates and reactivity. The derived decorator enables the creation of computed signals for class getters, recalculating values only when their dependencies change. An enumerable decorator offers control over property enumeration.

To manage the historical progression of values, the ValueHistory class in packages/state/src/history.ts tracks the current, initial, and previous states of a value. It supports custom equality checks and provides a reset mechanism, making it suitable for scenarios where tracking value changes over time is important.

Additionally, the system includes utilities for managing side effects, such as the effects function in packages/state/src/effects.ts, which aggregates multiple reactive effects into a single cleanup function. For creating non-reactive copies of objects, the snapshot function in packages/state/src/snapshot.ts captures the current state without establishing new reactive subscriptions. Memory management is aided by the WeakStore class in packages/state/src/store.ts, which uses WeakMap to store values weakly, allowing for garbage collection when keys are no longer referenced.

Core Reactive Primitives and Signal Management
The dnd-kit monorepo leverages @preact/signals-core to provide a robust and efficient reactive state management system. This system forms the foundation for tracking and responding to changes in drag and drop operations across different frameworks. The core reactive primitives, re-exported and extended within dnd-kit, enable a fine-grained approach to state updates, optimizing performance by minimizing unnecessary re-renders.

The fundamental reactive value is created using signal, which allows for declaring a piece of state that other parts of the application can observe. When a signal's value changes, any computations or side effects that depend on it are automatically re-evaluated. This automatic dependency tracking is crucial for building responsive user interfaces. The Signal and ReadonlySignal types provide clear interfaces for working with these reactive values, differentiating between mutable and immutable reactive states.

For managing side effects that occur when reactive values change, dnd-kit utilizes effect. This primitive allows developers to define functions that run automatically in response to signal updates. These effects can be used for tasks such as DOM manipulation, logging, or synchronization with external systems. To ensure proper resource management, effect functions can return a cleanup function, which is invoked when the effect is re-run or when its dependencies are no longer needed. This mechanism is further enhanced by effects in packages/state/src/effects.ts, which aggregates multiple cleanup functions into a single manageable unit.

Optimizing performance in reactive systems often involves controlling when updates are propagated. batch is employed to group multiple signal updates into a single transaction. This prevents intermediate states from triggering redundant re-evaluations, ensuring that observers only react once all batched updates have completed.

In scenarios where certain operations should not establish reactive dependencies, untracked is used. This primitive executes a block of code without registering any signals accessed within that block as dependencies. This is particularly useful for tasks like logging a signal's current value or performing computations that shouldn't trigger subsequent reactive updates. For instance, the snapshot function in packages/state/src/snapshot.ts utilizes untracked to create non-reactive copies of objects.

These primitives—signal, effect, batch, and untracked—provide a powerful and flexible toolkit for managing the dynamic state inherent in drag and drop interactions, ensuring both responsiveness and efficiency. Further details on how these are integrated into class-based components can be found in Reactive Decorators for Class Integration, and how specific computations are handled efficiently is covered in Enhanced Computed Signals and Deep Equality.

Enhanced Computed Signals and Deep Equality
typescript
import { computed } from '@dnd-kit/state/computed'; // Or similar path
import { deepEqual } from '@dnd-kit/state/comparators'; // Or similar path
import { signal } from '@preact/signals-core';

interface Item {
  id: string;
  value: number;
}

const sourceSignal = signal<Item[]>([{ id: 'a', value: 1 }]);

// Create a computed signal that uses deepEqual for comparison
const deepComputedSignal = computed(() => {
  // This array is a new reference each time, but its content might be deepEqual
  return sourceSignal.value.map(item => ({ ...item }));
}, deepEqual); // Only updates if the content is deeply different

let updateCount = 0;
deepComputedSignal.subscribe(() => {
  updateCount++;
});

sourceSignal.value = [{ id: 'a', value: 1 }]; // Content is deeply equal
sourceSignal.value = [{ id: 'a', value: 1 }]; // Content is still deeply equal

// updateCount will be 1, because the content didn't deeply change,
// even though the array reference was new.

sourceSignal.value = [{ id: 'a', value: 2 }]; // Content is deeply different, will trigger update
// updateCount will now be 2
The dnd-kit state management utilizes an enhanced computed function, building upon @preact/signals-core, to create reactive signals that can incorporate custom comparison logic. This enhancement is crucial for optimizing performance by preventing unnecessary re-evaluations and updates. By default, standard reactive signals might trigger updates even if a computed value's content is functionally the same but its reference has changed. The computed function, defined in packages/state/src/computed.ts, addresses this by allowing an optional comparator function.

The primary comparator used is deepEqual, found in packages/state/src/comparators.ts. This utility function performs a recursive deep equality comparison between two JavaScript values, handling primitives, functions, arrays, objects, and Sets. When a comparator like deepEqual is provided to the computed function, it is invoked to compare the newly computed value with its previous state. If deepEqual returns true, indicating that the values are considered equivalent despite potential reference differences, the signal avoids triggering an update. This mechanism ensures that downstream consumers only react to meaningful changes in the signal's content, thereby improving efficiency by reducing redundant computations and re-renders.

Reactive Decorators for Class Integration
typescript
class StateManager {
  // `reactive` makes `value` a signal, automatically updating consumers
  @reactive accessor value = 0;

  // `enumerable(false)` makes `internalCounter` non-enumerable
  @enumerable(false) accessor internalCounter = 0;

  // `derived` creates a computed signal for `doubledValue`
  // It reacts to changes in `value`
  @derived get doubledValue() {
    return this.value * 2;
  }

  // `enumerable(true)` (default) makes `increment` enumerable
  @enumerable()
  increment() {
    this.value++;
    this.internalCounter++;
  }
}

const state = new StateManager();

console.log(state.doubledValue); // Accesses the derived value

state.value = 5; // Changing `value` will update `doubledValue`

console.log(state.doubledValue);

state.increment(); // Calls the enumerable method

// Demonstrate enumerability
for (const key in state) {
  console.log(key); // `internalCounter` will not be logged
}
The dnd-kit state management system integrates Preact Signals into class properties through decorators, allowing for reactive behavior within class-based components. These decorators are defined in packages/state/src/decorators.ts.

The reactive decorator transforms a class property into a signal. When this property is accessed, it returns the signal's current value. When the property is assigned a new value, the signal's value is updated only if it differs from the current one, preventing unnecessary updates in reactive systems.

For properties that derive their values from other reactive states, the derived decorator can be applied to a class getter. This creates a computed signal that automatically re-evaluates its value only when its dependencies change, ensuring efficient updates for derived data.

The enumerable decorator provides control over the enumerability of a class field, getter, setter, accessor, or method. This is useful for managing the visibility and discoverability of class members, which can be important for serialization, debugging, or API design.

Value History Tracking and State Snapshotting
The ValueHistory class, defined in packages/state/src/history.ts, provides a mechanism for reactively tracking the progression of a value through its current, initial, and previous states. This class is particularly useful in scenarios where understanding the change over time for a specific value is crucial. It supports custom equality checks, allowing for flexible comparisons of complex objects beyond simple reference equality. When a new value is assigned, the system efficiently updates the current and previous states, leveraging batching and untracked operations from Preact Signals to optimize performance and prevent unnecessary re-evaluations.

Complementing value history tracking is the snapshot function, located in packages/state/src/snapshot.ts. This utility creates a non-reactive, shallow copy of an object. The primary purpose of snapshot is to capture an object's state at a particular moment without creating new reactive subscriptions. This is essential for operations such as serialization, debugging, or passing data to components that do not need to react to changes in the original object, thereby preventing unintended side effects or re-renders within a reactive context. The untracked function from @preact/signals-core is central to this process, ensuring that property access during the copying mechanism does not establish new dependencies.

Effect Aggregation and Weak-Referenced Stores
The dnd-kit package includes mechanisms for managing reactive side effects and memory efficiently. The effects utility function within packages/state/src/effects.ts provides a way to combine multiple reactive side effects into a single, manageable cleanup function. This aggregation simplifies resource management by allowing developers to group related effects and dispose of them simultaneously, helping to prevent memory leaks or unintended ongoing operations.

For managing data storage in a garbage-collectible manner, the WeakStore class, defined in packages/state/src/store.ts, is utilized. This class implements a nested key-value store using a WeakMap for its primary keys. This design ensures that if a key object is no longer referenced elsewhere in the application, its associated data in the store becomes eligible for garbage collection. This prevents memory leaks that can occur when references to stored data persist even after the corresponding key objects are no longer in use, enhancing the overall memory efficiency of the toolkit.

Helper Utilities
typescript
// Example: Reordering a simple array using arrayMove
const simpleArray = ['Item 1', 'Item 2', 'Item 3'];
const movedSimpleArray = arrayMove(simpleArray, 0, 2);
// movedSimpleArray will be ['Item 2', 'Item 3', 'Item 1']

// Example: Reordering a grouped collection using move
const groupedItems = {
  GroupA: ['A1', 'A2', 'A3'],
  GroupB: ['B1', 'B2'],
};

// Mock a drag event for moving 'A1' from GroupA to the end of GroupB
// (A real event would come from dnd-kit's dragover or dragend handlers)
const mockDragEvent = {
  operation: {
    source: { id: 'A1', initialIndex: 0, initialGroup: 'GroupA', index: 2, group: 'GroupB' }, // Represents the dragged item
    target: { id: 'B2' }, // Represents the drop target
    canceled: false,
  },
  // Other event properties would be here (e.g., preventDefault for dragover)
};

const movedGroupedItems = move(groupedItems, mockDragEvent);
/*
movedGroupedItems will be:
{
  GroupA: ['A2', 'A3'],
  GroupB: ['B1', 'B2', 'A1'],
}
*/
The dnd-kit repository includes a collection of framework-agnostic utility functions designed to streamline common data transformations during drag-and-drop interactions. These helpers, found primarily within the packages/helpers directory, facilitate operations such as reordering and swapping items within various data structures, including simple arrays and grouped collections. They are built to support features like optimistic UI updates and robust lookup strategies.

At its core, the helpers package provides functions for fundamental array manipulation: arrayMove reinserts an element from one position to another within an array, and arraySwap exchanges two elements by their indices. Both functions are designed to return a new array, ensuring the immutability of the original data.

Building upon these primitives, higher-level functions such as move and swap orchestrate drag-and-drop reordering. These functions accept the current collection of items and a drag event object to intelligently determine how items should be reordered or swapped. They handle complexities such as identifying items by unique identifiers, reconciling optimistic updates where the UI might anticipate a change before it's fully committed, and falling back to index-based logic when IDs are dynamically generated or unavailable. For grouped collections (e.g., records where keys represent groups and values are arrays of items), these functions also manage transfers between groups. The CHANGELOG.md for this package, located at packages/helpers/CHANGELOG.md, details enhancements, such as improved support for computed sortable IDs and optimistic sorting reconciliation, reflecting the continuous refinement of these utilities.

Core Array Manipulation (arrayMove and arraySwap)
typescript
function arrayMove<T extends any[]>(array: T, from: number, to: number): T {
  // ... (implementation details - creates a new array)
}

function arraySwap<T extends any[]>(array: T, from: number, to: number): T {
  // ... (implementation details - creates a new array)
}

const colors = ['red', 'green', 'blue', 'yellow'];

// Using arrayMove to change the order of items
const movedColors = arrayMove(colors, 1, 3);
// movedColors is now ['red', 'blue', 'yellow', 'green']
// colors remains ['red', 'green', 'blue', 'yellow']

// Using arraySwap to exchange two items
const swappedColors = arraySwap(colors, 0, 2);
// swappedColors is now ['blue', 'green', 'red', 'yellow']
// colors remains ['red', 'green', 'blue', 'yellow']

// The original array is not mutated
console.log('Original colors:', colors);
console.log('Colors after arrayMove:', movedColors);
console.log('Colors after arraySwap:', swappedColors);
The dnd-kit toolkit provides fundamental, immutable array manipulation functions: arrayMove and arraySwap. These functions are foundational for reordering and exchanging elements within arrays, respectively, and serve as core building blocks for higher-level drag-and-drop operations while preserving data immutability. The arrayMove function reinserts an element from one index to another within an array, shifting intermediate elements as needed. Conversely, arraySwap exchanges two elements in an array by their indices. Both functions are designed to return new arrays, thereby ensuring that the original arrays remain unchanged. These utilities are re-exported through packages/helpers/src/index.ts for convenient access, with their core logic residing in packages/helpers/src/move.ts. Their immutability is crucial for predictable state management in reactive UIs.

High-Level Drag-and-Drop Reordering (move and swap)
The move and swap functions, exposed through packages/helpers/src/index.ts, provide high-level abstractions for reordering and exchanging items in response to drag-and-drop events. These functions process drag-and-drop event data, intelligently applying either movement or swapping semantics to collections of items.

The core logic for these operations is encapsulated within an internal mutate function, detailed in packages/helpers/src/move.ts. This function is capable of handling both flat arrays and grouped collections (e.g., Record<UniqueIdentifier, Items>). It performs advanced reconciliation, using item ids for lookup to determine the source and target positions for a dragged item. In cases where id-based lookup is not feasible, such as with computed IDs, a fallback mechanism uses initialIndex and index properties from the drag operation's source object.

For grouped collections, mutate navigates through different groups to identify the correct sourceIndex, sourceParent, targetIndex, and targetParent, facilitating cross-group transfers. It also addresses optimistic updates, where the source.index might deviate from the actual array index due to prior UI manipulations. The position of the dragged item, derived from dragOperation.shape?.current.center or dragOperation.position.current, guides the determination of insertion points when a direct target is not immediately available. The underlying array manipulations are performed by immutable functions arrayMove and arraySwap, which return new arrays without modifying the originals. These functions are further detailed in [Core Array Manipulation (arrayMove and arraySwap)](#helper-utilities-core-array-manipulation-arraymove-and-arrayswap).

Testing and Validation of Helper Utilities
typescript
describe('arrayMove', () => {
  it('should not mutate the original array', () => {
    const arr = ['a', 'b', 'c'];
    // Call arrayMove, which returns a new array
    arrayMove(arr, 0, 2);
    // Assert that the original array remains unchanged (immutability)
    expect(arr).toEqual(['a', 'b', 'c']);
  });
});

describe('move – flat array, optimistic reconciliation', () => {
  it('should reconcile when source.index differs from ID-found position', () => {
    const items = ['a', 'b', 'c', 'd'];
    // Simulates dragend after optimistic sorting:
    // source.id === target.id, items not yet updated, source.index = optimistic position
    const source = mockSortableSource({
      id: 'a',
      initialIndex: 0,
      index: 2, // The optimistic position
    });
    // target.id === source.id (optimistic sorting sets drop target to source)
    const event = dragOverEvent(source, mockTarget('a'));
    // source 'a' is found at index 0 in 'items', but source.index is 2.
    // The `move` function reconciles this, effectively moving 'a' from 0 to 2.
    expect(move(items, event)).toEqual(['b', 'c', 'a', 'd']);
  });
});
The comprehensive testing strategy for the helper utilities is primarily detailed in packages/helpers/tests/move.test.ts. This file validates the correctness, immutability, optimistic reconciliation, and fallback mechanisms of the arrayMove, arraySwap, move, and swap functions across various scenarios, including edge cases and event handling.

The tests confirm the immutability of the core array manipulation functions, ensuring that arrayMove and arraySwap return new arrays without altering the originals. This is a critical aspect for predictable state management in reactive UIs. For more information on array manipulation, refer to [Core Array Manipulation (arrayMove and arraySwap)](#helper-utilities-core-array-manipulation-arraymove-and-arrayswap).

Validation for the higher-level move and swap functions covers their ability to intelligently reorder and swap items in both flat and grouped collections, utilizing ID-based lookups. The testing suite confirms optimistic reconciliation, which is essential for providing smooth user experiences during drag-and-drop operations, allowing the UI to update immediately based on anticipated changes. Furthermore, the tests verify the fallback mechanisms for scenarios involving computed IDs or when explicit IDs are not available, ensuring robustness across different integration patterns. These functions are designed to operate by processing drag events, with tests confirming appropriate preventDefault calls when no actual changes occur. For a detailed discussion on these functions, see [High-Level Drag-and-Drop Reordering (move and swap)](#helper-utilities-high-level-drag-and-drop-reordering-move-and-swap).

Framework Adapters (React, SolidJS, Svelte, Vue)
Component/Hook	React	SolidJS	Svelte	Vue
DragDropProvider	react/src/core/context/DragDropProvider.tsx	solid/src/core/context/DragDropProvider.tsx	vue/src/core/context/DragDropProvider.ts	vue/src/core/context/DragDropProvider.ts
useDraggable / createDraggable	react/src/core/draggable/useDraggable.ts	solid/src/core/draggable/useDraggable.ts	svelte/src/core/draggable/createDraggable.svelte.ts	vue/src/core/draggable/useDraggable.ts
useDroppable / createDroppable	react/src/core/droppable/useDroppable.ts	solid/src/core/droppable/useDroppable.ts	svelte/src/core/droppable/createDroppable.svelte.ts	vue/src/core/droppable/useDroppable.ts
useSortable / createSortable	react/src/sortable/useSortable.ts	solid/src/sortable/useSortable.ts	svelte/src/sortable/createSortable.svelte.ts	vue/src/sortable/useSortable.ts
useDeepSignal / createDeepSignal	react/src/hooks/useDeepSignal.ts	solid/src/hooks/useDeepSignal.ts	svelte/src/utilities/createDeepSignal.svelte.ts	vue/src/composables/useDeepSignal.ts
The @dnd-kit library offers framework-specific adapters to facilitate the integration of its drag-and-drop functionalities into various web environments. These adapters provide components and hooks tailored for React, SolidJS, Svelte, and Vue, enabling developers to build lightweight, performant, and extensible drag-and-drop interfaces by leveraging the core DOM implementation. Each adapter is designed to align with the idiomatic patterns and reactivity models of its respective framework.

For instance, the React Adapter: Core Components and Hooks utilizes components like DragDropProvider and DragOverlay, and hooks such as useDraggable and useDroppable, to offer a declarative API. The DragDropProvider component, defined in packages/react/src/core/context/DragDropProvider.tsx, acts as the central wrapper, managing the DragDropManager instance and registering lifecycle event listeners. Similarly, the SolidJS Adapter: Core Features and Reactive Primitives provides its own DragDropProvider in packages/solid/src/core/context/DragDropProvider.tsx and hooks like useDraggable in packages/solid/src/core/draggable/useDraggable.ts, leveraging Solid.js's fine-grained reactivity. The Svelte adapter, as detailed in Svelte Adapter: Runes, Attachments, and Deep Reactivity, makes use of Svelte 5's runes and attachments through functions like createDraggable in packages/svelte/src/core/draggable/createDraggable.svelte.ts. The Vue Adapter: Composables, Components, and Deep Reactivity uses composables such as useDraggable from packages/vue/src/core/draggable/useDraggable.ts and the DragDropProvider component from packages/vue/src/core/context/DragDropProvider.ts to integrate with Vue's reactivity system.

All adapters build upon the core DOM implementation of dnd-kit, abstracting away direct DOM manipulation. They also integrate sortable functionalities, allowing elements within lists to be reordered via drag and drop. For instance, useSortable is available in the React adapter through packages/react/src/sortable/useSortable.ts, in SolidJS via packages/solid/src/sortable/useSortable.ts, in Svelte as createSortable via packages/svelte/src/sortable/createSortable.svelte.ts, and in Vue as useSortable via packages/vue/src/sortable/useSortable.ts. These sortable hooks and functions manage the underlying Sortable instances, synchronize properties, and provide reactive state for components.

A common pattern across these adapters is the use of deep reactivity utilities, such as useDeepSignal in both React (packages/react/src/hooks/useDeepSignal.ts) and SolidJS (packages/solid/src/hooks/useDeepSignal.ts), createDeepSignal in Svelte (packages/svelte/src/utilities/createDeepSignal.svelte.ts), and useDeepSignal in Vue (packages/vue/src/composables/useDeepSignal.ts). These utilities ensure that changes to nested properties of configuration objects or state variables trigger appropriate updates within the framework's reactivity system, optimizing performance by preventing unnecessary re-renders. The CHANGELOG.md files for each package, such as packages/react/CHANGELOG.md, packages/solid/CHANGELOG.md, and packages/svelte/CHANGELOG.md, document the evolution of these adapters, including the introduction of configurable plugins, sensors, and modifiers to enhance flexibility.

React Adapter: Core Components and Hooks
The React adapter for dnd-kit offers a declarative API for embedding drag-and-drop features into React applications. It integrates with React's component and hook paradigms, building upon the core DOM-based drag-and-drop implementation to manage state, events, and rendering.

The DragDropProvider, defined in packages/react/src/core/context/DragDropProvider.tsx, serves as the central component for establishing a drag-and-drop environment. It wraps the parts of an application that require drag-and-drop functionality, providing a DragDropManager instance to all descendant components via React's Context API. This provider orchestrates drag-and-drop operations, configuring the DragDropManager with plugins, modifiers, and sensors, and registering event listeners for various lifecycle events such as beforedragstart, dragstart, dragmove, dragover, dragend, and collision. These events trigger corresponding callbacks passed as props to the DragDropProvider, enabling React components to react to the drag-and-drop process. The DragDropContext, defined in packages/react/src/core/context/context.ts, is responsible for managing the DragDropManager instance. For advanced use cases, a Renderer component, detailed in packages/react/src/core/context/renderer.ts, facilitates tracking of asynchronous rendering transitions within event callbacks.

To make a component draggable, the useDraggable hook, found in packages/react/src/core/draggable/useDraggable.ts, is employed. This hook manages a @dnd-kit/dom Draggable instance, synchronizing its properties with React state and providing handleRef and ref setters for associating the DOM elements that initiate or form the draggable item. It exposes reactive state variables like isDragging, isDropping, and isDragSource, which allow components to dynamically update their UI based on the draggable's status. For visual feedback during an active drag operation, the DragOverlay component, located in packages/react/src/core/draggable/DragOverlay.tsx, renders a custom visual representation of the dragged item. This overlay supports custom children and allows control over drop animations. Its design ensures it remains purely visual, preventing nested elements from inadvertently registering with the main drag-and-drop system by patching the DragDropManager's registration methods.

For designating elements as drop targets, the useDroppable hook, defined in packages/react/src/core/droppable/useDroppable.ts, is used. This hook initializes a @dnd-kit/dom Droppable instance, synchronizing properties such as id, element, acceptance rules, collisionDetector, and type. It returns the tracked Droppable instance, an isDropTarget getter, and a ref callback for assigning the target DOM element. The useDeepSignal hook from packages/react/src/hooks/useDeepSignal.ts is utilized to ensure reactive updates when the Droppable instance's properties change, leading to efficient re-rendering of dependent components.

These components and hooks rely on internal React hooks, such as useStableInstance for ensuring manager instance stability, useLatest for obtaining current callback references, and useOnValueChange for updating the DragDropManager when configurations like plugins, sensors, or modifiers change. The useInstance hook, located in packages/react/src/core/hooks/useInstance.ts, is crucial for managing the lifecycle and registration of Draggable or Droppable instances with the DragDropManager, guaranteeing proper initialization and cleanup. For further details on how these instances interact with the DragDropManager, refer to Drag and Drop Manager Lifecycle and Actions.

React Adapter: Sortable Functionality and Utility Hooks
dnd-kit implements sortable functionality within React by adapting the core drag-and-drop mechanics to the React component model, building upon the @dnd-kit/dom/sortable module. This integration primarily occurs through the useSortable hook, defined in packages/react/src/sortable/useSortable.ts, which allows React components to declare themselves as sortable items. The useSortable hook is exported via the sortable entry point in packages/react/src/sortable/index.ts, alongside utility functions like isSortable and isSortableOperation that aid in identifying sortable components and operations.

The useSortable hook manages the lifecycle of a Sortable instance from @dnd-kit/dom/sortable, synchronizing its properties with React component state. It receives various input parameters, including id, group, index, and type, which define the sortable item's identity and behavior. The hook internally leverages several utility hooks to achieve this synchronization and manage reactivity. For instance, useDeepSignal, from packages/react/src/hooks/useDeepSignal.ts, creates a reactive proxy around the Sortable instance. This proxy ensures that any changes to the Sortable instance's properties automatically trigger re-renders in the React component that uses the useSortable hook, maintaining a reactive UI. This is crucial for reflecting the visual changes during a drag operation, such as an item changing its position.

Property synchronization with the underlying Sortable instance is handled by hooks like useOnValueChange, found in packages/react/src/hooks/useOnValueChange.ts, and useOnElementChange, found in packages/react/src/hooks/useOnElementChange.ts. useOnValueChange detects when a primitive or object property (like id or group) changes and updates the Sortable instance accordingly, using deepEqual for complex objects to prevent unnecessary updates. useOnElementChange monitors changes in the actual DOM element associated with the sortable item, updating the Sortable instance's element and handle properties when the underlying DOM node changes. This ensures that the Sortable instance always interacts with the correct DOM elements, even if they are dynamically rendered or replaced.

Performance and correct timing in the React lifecycle are addressed by useIsomorphicLayoutEffect, as detailed in packages/react/src/hooks/useIsomorphicLayoutEffect.ts. This hook conditionally uses React's useLayoutEffect on the client side and useEffect on the server, ensuring that DOM manipulations, such as updating element references or refreshing shapes, occur at the appropriate time without causing visual inconsistencies or warnings during server-side rendering. For example, useIsomorphicLayoutEffect with batch ensures that group and index updates are processed simultaneously, preventing intermediate inconsistent states.

The useSortable hook also provides various ref callbacks (handleRef, ref, sourceRef, targetRef) that allow developers to assign DOM elements to the Sortable instance, which is critical for defining what parts of the component are draggable and what serves as its visual representation. These ref callbacks are memoized to optimize performance and prevent premature cleanup of the Sortable instance during drag operations. Further details on how general utility hooks like useConstant, useLatest, and useComputed contribute to overall React adapter functionality can be found in React Adapter: Core Components and Hooks and Helper Utilities.

SolidJS Adapter: Core Features and Reactive Primitives
The SolidJS adapter integrates dnd-kit's core drag-and-drop features into Solid.js applications, leveraging Solid.js's fine-grained reactivity to manage state and DOM updates efficiently. This integration is centered around key components and hooks that mirror the foundational abstractions of dnd-kit.

The DragDropProvider component, defined in packages/solid/src/core/context/DragDropProvider.tsx, acts as the central orchestrator for all drag-and-drop operations. It instantiates the DragDropManager (which handles the core logic as described in Drag and Drop Manager Lifecycle and Actions), subscribes to its lifecycle events (such as drag start, move, and end), and triggers user-defined callbacks. A crucial aspect of the DragDropProvider is its use of the createSaveElementPosition utility from packages/solid/src/utilities/saveElementPosition.ts to address Solid.js rendering nuances, particularly for sortable elements. This utility saves and restores an element's DOM position, ensuring visual stability during drag operations. The DragDropProvider also uses a DragDropContext from packages/solid/src/core/context/context.ts to make the DragDropManager instance accessible to all descendant components without prop drilling.

To make elements draggable, the adapter provides the useDraggable hook, found in packages/solid/src/core/draggable/useDraggable.ts. This hook allows developers to associate an element with draggable behavior by configuring its properties like id, disabled state, and various plugins, modifiers, and sensors Extensibility: Plugins, Modifiers, and Sensors. It returns reactive state indicating whether the element is currently being dragged or is a drag source, along with reference setters to link to the actual DOM element. Visual feedback during a drag is handled by the DragOverlay component, located in packages/solid/src/core/draggable/DragOverlay.tsx. This component renders a customizable visual representation of the dragged item, distinct from its original position. It supports various dropAnimation configurations and can be conditionally rendered. Importantly, the DragOverlay uses a patched DragDropManager to prevent its internal components from interfering with the main drag-and-drop registry.

For defining areas where draggable items can be dropped, the useDroppable hook from packages/solid/src/core/droppable/useDroppable.ts is used. This hook registers Droppable instances (see Entity Management: Draggable and Droppable) with the DragDropManager, allowing them to accept or reject drops based on configured id, accept criteria, and collisionDetector. It exposes reactive state to indicate if the droppable area is currently a target for a dragged item.

A key aspect of the SolidJS adapter's efficiency is its use of useDeepSignal, defined in packages/solid/src/hooks/useDeepSignal.ts. This custom hook provides deep reactivity for Solid.js signals that contain objects. Unlike standard signals that only react to changes in object references, useDeepSignal ensures that components re-render when any nested property within the object changes. This is achieved by wrapping the object in a JavaScript Proxy to track accessed properties and using Solid.js's createEffect to observe these properties for changes, thus optimizing performance by only reacting to relevant data mutations. The SolidJS adapter also provides utility hooks like useDragDropManager, useDragDropMonitor, useDragOperation, and useInstance in packages/solid/src/core/hooks to provide granular access and control over the DragDropManager's state and lifecycle within Solid.js components.

SolidJS Adapter: Sortable Functionality
solid
function SortableItem(props: {id: string | number}) {
  const {
    setNodeRef, // The ref to set on the actual DOM element
    attributes, // HTML attributes to spread on the sortable element for accessibility
    listeners, // Event listeners to spread on the handle or the sortable element itself
    transform, // The CSS transform to apply to the sortable element
    transition, // The CSS transition to apply to the sortable element
    isDragging, // Whether the item is currently being dragged
  } = useSortable({id: props.id});

  const style = {
    transition,
    transform: transform ? `translate3d(${transform.x}px, ${transform.y}px, 0)` : undefined,
    // Add other styling as needed
  };

  return (
    <div ref={setNodeRef} style={style} {...attributes} {...listeners}>
      {/* Your sortable item content */}
      Item {props.id} {isDragging ? '(Dragging)' : ''}
    </div>
  );
}

function MySortableComponent() {
  const items = ['1', '2', '3']; // Example sortable items

  return (
    <div>
      {items.map((id) => (
        <SortableItem key={id} id={id} />
      ))}
    </div>
  );
}
The SolidJS adapter extends the core drag and drop functionality to include sortable capabilities, allowing components to define orderable lists. This integration is primarily managed through the useSortable hook, which acts as a bridge between Solid.js's reactivity system and the underlying sortable logic provided by @dnd-kit/dom/sortable.

The useSortable hook is responsible for managing instances of the Sortable class. It initializes a Sortable object and ensures that its properties, such as id, disabled, alignment, plugins, modifiers, and sensors, are kept in sync with the input provided to the hook. This synchronization leverages Solid.js primitives like createSignal, createEffect, on, and batch to efficiently manage updates. The hook also provides setters for DOM element references (ref, handleRef, sourceRef, targetRef), allowing developers to link specific HTML elements to the sortable instance.

To provide reactive feedback on the sortable item's state, such as whether it is currently being dragged or is a drop target, the useSortable hook utilizes a deep reactive signal for the Sortable instance. This is achieved through useDeepSignal, which tracks changes within the object's properties, not just changes to the object reference itself. This enables components to react to the isDragging, isDropping, isDragSource, and isDropTarget states. Additionally, the hook ensures that the visual shape of the sortable item is updated correctly by calling sortable.refreshShape when its index changes and no drag operation is active.

Further information about the overall SolidJS adapter can be found in SolidJS Adapter: Core Features and Reactive Primitives.

Svelte Adapter: Runes, Attachments, and Deep Reactivity
The Svelte adapter for dnd-kit integrates the toolkit's drag-and-drop functionality with Svelte 5's reactivity model, leveraging runes ($effect, $state) and attachments for an idiomatic developer experience. This integration allows for the creation of reactive draggable and droppable elements, as well as sortable lists, all while maintaining efficient state management.

The adapter provides a <DragDropProvider> component that establishes the drag-and-drop context, managing sensors, plugins, and dispatching events for its child components. Within this context, the createDraggable function enables any HTML element to become draggable. It returns a reactive object exposing the draggable element's state, such as whether it is currently dragging, and an attach function for binding the draggable behavior to a DOM node using Svelte's {@attach} directive. Similarly, createDroppable defines drop target areas, returning a reactive object to indicate if it is a current drop target and an attach function for DOM binding. For sortable interactions, createSortable extends these capabilities, enabling elements within a list to be reordered through drag and drop.

A key aspect of this adapter is its deep reactivity, which is managed through createDeepSignal in packages/svelte/src/utilities/createDeepSignal.svelte.ts. This utility bridges @dnd-kit/state signals with Svelte 5's reactivity system. It creates a deeply reactive proxy for objects, ensuring that changes to nested properties of the underlying dnd-kit instances (like Draggable or Sortable) automatically trigger updates in Svelte components. This is achieved by using a hybrid push-pull mechanism: a Proxy tracks accessed properties, and a Svelte $effect listens for changes to these tracked properties, incrementing a $state variable to trigger Svelte's reactivity efficiently. This approach optimizes performance by avoiding unnecessary re-renders for unused parts of a deep object while providing comprehensive deep reactivity.

Event monitoring is handled by createDragDropMonitor in packages/svelte/src/core/hooks/createDragDropMonitor.svelte.ts, which registers and cleans up global drag-and-drop event handlers using Svelte's $effect lifecycle hook. This ensures that event listeners are correctly managed within Svelte's reactivity system. The createInstance utility in packages/svelte/src/core/hooks/createInstance.svelte.ts manages the lifecycle of dnd-kit instances, ensuring proper initialization and cleanup within Svelte's reactive context. See Drag and Drop Manager Lifecycle and Actions for more information on the core DragDropManager lifecycle.

Vue Adapter: Composables, Components, and Deep Reactivity
The @dnd-kit/vue package integrates dnd-kit's drag-and-drop functionality into Vue.js applications, leveraging Vue's reactivity system to provide a declarative API. This integration is achieved through a combination of Vue components and composables that manage the drag-and-drop lifecycle, synchronize state, and facilitate DOM manipulation.

The central component for establishing a drag-and-drop context is DragDropProvider, located at packages/vue/src/core/context/DragDropProvider.ts. This component initializes and manages a DragDropManager instance, which orchestrates all drag-and-drop operations. It registers event listeners for the manager's lifecycle events, such as beforeDragStart, dragStart, dragMove, dragOver, dragEnd, and collision. These events are then propagated as Vue custom events, allowing developers to react to drag-and-drop interactions within their Vue components. DragDropProvider also utilizes a renderer (from packages/vue/src/core/context/renderer.ts) to synchronize DragDropManager operations with Vue's rendering and CSS transitions, ensuring smooth visual updates during dragging. The DragDropManager instance is made available to descendant components through Vue's context system, managed by provideDragDropContext and injectDragDropContext from packages/vue/src/core/context/context.ts. For a deeper understanding of the DragDropManager's lifecycle and event architecture, refer to Drag and Drop Manager Lifecycle and Actions.

To make individual elements draggable, the useDraggable composable, defined in packages/vue/src/core/draggable/useDraggable.ts, is employed. This composable wraps the core Draggable class from @dnd-kit/dom, providing a reactive interface for managing draggable element properties such as its associated DOM element, handle, ID, and disabled state. It exposes reactive state indicators like isDragging and isDropping, allowing Vue components to update their UI based on the draggable's status. During a drag operation, visual feedback is provided by the DragOverlay component (packages/vue/src/core/draggable/DragOverlay.ts). A key feature of DragOverlay is its ability to display a visual representation of the dragged item without allowing its children to be registered as droppable targets. This is achieved by creating a patchedManager that overrides the register and unregister methods of the DragDropManager's registry, effectively preventing self-dropping or incorrect nesting.

For defining areas where draggable items can be dropped, the useDroppable composable (packages/vue/src/core/droppable/useDroppable.ts) is used. This composable integrates dnd-kit's Droppable class, enabling developers to configure drop targets with properties like element, id, accept criteria, and collisionDetector. It exposes an isDropTarget computed property, which reactively indicates if the droppable element is currently an active drop target. The core Draggable and Droppable entities, along with their properties and interaction with the DragDropManager, are further detailed in Entity Management: Draggable and Droppable.

The @dnd-kit/vue package also extends its functionality to support sortable lists through the useSortable composable (packages/vue/src/sortable/useSortable.ts). This composable builds upon the Sortable class from @dnd-kit/dom/sortable to provide reactive management of sortable items. It synchronizes properties like the element, handle, source, and target, and exposes reactive computed properties such as isDragging, isDropping, isDragSource, and isDropTarget to reflect the sortable item's state.

A crucial aspect of the @dnd-kit/vue adapter is its approach to deep reactivity, particularly for managing complex state objects that may contain nested properties. The useDeepSignal composable, found in packages/vue/src/composables/useDeepSignal.ts, enables deep reactivity for objects or primitives. Unlike standard Vue reactivity which might only track changes to the top-level object reference, useDeepSignal ensures that changes to nested properties within an object also trigger re-computations. This is achieved by using a Proxy to track accessed properties and an internal effect to monitor their values, forcing re-evaluation when a nested change occurs.

Furthermore, the adapter leverages Vue's native reactivity features like watchEffect for efficient property synchronization and toValueDeep (from packages/vue/src/utilities/ref.ts) for resolving nested reactive references within objects or arrays into their plain values. The unrefElement utility (packages/vue/src/utilities/element.ts) is also vital for safely extracting raw DOM elements from Vue component instances or reactive references, ensuring that dnd-kit's core DOM-based functionalities can interact correctly with Vue's virtual DOM.

Interactive Examples and Storybook
The dnd-kit monorepo provides a comprehensive set of interactive examples and demonstrations across various frameworks through its Storybook applications, which are integral for development, testing, and showcasing the toolkit's capabilities. This includes dedicated Storybook environments for React (in apps/stories), SolidJS (in apps/stories-solid), Svelte (in apps/stories-svelte), Vue (in apps/stories-vue), and vanilla JavaScript (in apps/stories-vanilla). These Storybooks are not merely static documentation; they are dynamic, interactive playgrounds that allow developers to experiment with draggable, droppable, sortable, and resizable components in real-time.

The core setup for these Storybook instances is consistent, leveraging shared configurations, global styling, and dynamic theming to ensure a uniform experience across different frameworks. For example, common UI components like Container and Item are defined as custom web components in apps/stories-shared/components, and their associated styles (draggableStyles, droppableStyles, sortableStyles) are aggregated in apps/stories-shared/styles/sandbox.ts. These shared resources maintain a consistent visual and functional baseline for all examples. The global setup, managed by apps/stories-shared/register.ts, injects these shared CSS styles and registers the custom web components, while also enabling dynamic theming (e.g., dark mode) based on URL parameters.

Each framework-specific Storybook meticulously details how to implement dnd-kit functionalities. For instance, the React Storybook contains extensive examples for Draggable components, demonstrating various configurations like DragHandle, DragOverlay, and different Sensor types (e.g., PointerSensor, KeyboardSensor), as well as various Modifiers for restricting movement. Similarly, Droppable examples showcase interactions with single and multiple drop targets, while Sortable examples cover a wide array of list types, including horizontal, vertical, grid, and even multiple interconnected lists, often integrating features like drag handles, cloning, and custom transitions. The Vue, Svelte, and SolidJS Storybooks mirror this comprehensive approach, adapting the examples to their native component models and reactivity systems.

A key feature enhancing the interactivity and utility of these examples is the @dnd-kit/storybook-addon-codesandbox package, located in packages/storybook-addon-codesandbox. This Storybook addon allows users to export the code of any Storybook example directly to online development environments like CodeSandbox or StackBlitz. It intelligently collects the necessary story files, generates appropriate package.json and index.js (or index.tsx) files, and then programmatically creates or opens a sandbox. This functionality is accessible both from the Storybook manager UI (via an IconButton) and directly within the preview decorators, enabling seamless transitions from a live demo to an editable code environment.

Beyond the Storybooks, the documentation in apps/docs also integrates interactive CodeSandbox examples using Sandpack components. This allows users to view and modify code snippets directly within the documentation, providing a dynamic learning experience. The script in apps/docs/sandpack.js dynamically embeds these Sandpack components, configuring dependencies, applying themes, and customizing their appearance with CSS for a cohesive visual integration.

Cross-Framework Storybook Ecosystem
The dnd-kit repository employs a unified Storybook ecosystem to demonstrate its drag-and-drop functionalities across multiple JavaScript frameworks including React, SolidJS, Svelte, Vue, and vanilla JavaScript. This ecosystem is designed for consistency in presentation and behavior, allowing developers to interact with examples that reflect real-world usage.

At the core of this cross-framework consistency are shared configurations and components residing in the apps/stories-shared directory. This directory provides reusable UI elements like Container and Item through custom web components (container-component and item-component), which are registered globally via apps/stories-shared/register.ts. These components ensure a uniform visual and structural foundation for all drag-and-drop examples, regardless of the underlying framework. Similarly, global CSS styles, including draggableStyles, droppableStyles, and sortableStyles, are defined in apps/stories-shared/styles/sandbox.ts and injected universally. This approach standardizes the appearance and behavior of draggable and droppable elements across all Storybook instances, maintaining visual feedback for DND states such as active, hovered, or disabled elements.

Each framework-specific Storybook application—found in directories like apps/stories (React), apps/stories-solid (SolidJS), apps/stories-svelte (Svelte), and apps/stories-vue (Vue), as well as apps/stories-vanilla for vanilla JS—integrates these shared resources. This allows for framework-native implementations of dnd-kit components to leverage the common styling and structure, while adapting to their respective reactivity models and component architectures. For instance, @dnd-kit/storybook-addon-codesandbox is integrated into each Storybook's configuration file (e.g., apps/stories/.storybook/main.ts), providing interactive code export functionality. This enables users to directly open and experiment with any example in CodeSandbox or StackBlitz, complete with pre-configured dependencies and source code. Dynamic theming is also supported, allowing the document.body to switch between dark and light modes based on URL query parameters, affecting the appearance of components and the overall Storybook interface.

The documentation also benefits from this integrated approach, as described in Documentation with Interactive Code Examples. This ensures that the interactive examples presented in the documentation are consistent with those in the Storybook, reinforcing a cohesive learning and development experience.

Shared Components and Styling Across Storybooks
Within the dnd-kit monorepo, a collection of reusable components and styling utilities, located in the apps/stories-shared directory, is used to ensure a consistent look and feel across various Storybook examples. This approach standardizes visual presentation and interaction for draggable and droppable elements throughout different framework implementations.

The shared components include a Container and an Item, both implemented as custom web components. The Container component, defined in apps/stories-shared/components/Container/Container.ts, provides a semantic wrapper for grouping related content within Storybook examples. Its styling, found in apps/stories-shared/components/Container/Container.css, establishes flexible layouts, visual feedback for interactive states (such as shadow effects when a drag operation is active), and thematic adjustments for dark mode. Similarly, the Item component, defined in apps/stories-shared/components/Item/Item.ts, serves as a fundamental building block for individual draggable or display elements. Its associated styling in apps/stories-shared/components/Item/Item.css covers base appearance, focus states, accessibility attributes, and visual cues during drag operations. These custom elements provide a consistent base for all DND-related stories regardless of the framework.

Beyond structural components, the dnd-kit monorepo also provides aggregated CSS styles designed for direct application to HTML elements. These styles, located in apps/stories-shared/styles/sandbox.ts, include draggableStyles, droppableStyles, handleStyles, sortableStyles, and multipleListsStyles. These style definitions encapsulate the visual aspects of drag-and-drop interactions, such as the appearance of draggable buttons, droppable containers, drag handles, and sortable lists. By using these predefined CSS strings, the Storybook examples maintain a uniform aesthetic and behavior across React, SolidJS, Svelte, Vue, and vanilla JavaScript implementations. This centralized styling ensures that visual feedback and interaction cues are consistent, enhancing the user's understanding of dnd-kit's capabilities across its diverse framework integrations.

Interactive Code Export to CodeSandbox and StackBlitz
The @dnd-kit/storybook-addon-codesandbox package provides functionality to export Storybook examples directly to online development environments like CodeSandbox or StackBlitz. This enables users to interact with, modify, and share code snippets in a live, executable environment. The addon integrates with Storybook's manager UI and can also display buttons within story previews for immediate access.

At its core, the addon operates by collecting files associated with a Storybook story, generating necessary configuration files, and then programmatically initiating the creation or opening of a sandbox on the chosen platform. The process involves identifying the story's source code and other files defined in its parameters. A package.json file is generated, consolidating dependencies and dev dependencies from both global and story-specific configurations. Additionally, an index.js (or index.tsx) entry file is created to correctly bootstrap the sandbox environment, often importing the main story file. These operations are handled by functions such as collectFiles in packages/storybook-addon-codesandbox/src/collect-files.ts, which merges file definitions and generates essential project metadata.

The addon offers two primary integration points within Storybook. First, a tool in the Storybook manager UI (implemented in packages/storybook-addon-codesandbox/src/manager.tsx) allows users to trigger sandbox creation for the currently viewed story. This tool uses Storybook hooks to access story data and parameters, and upon activation, it calls external functions like openStackBlitz or createSandbox/openSandbox (defined in packages/storybook-addon-codesandbox/src/define.ts) to handle the communication with CodeSandbox or StackBlitz APIs. Second, preview decorators (found in packages/storybook-addon-codesandbox/src/preview) inject an "Open Sandbox" button directly into the story's preview area. A React-specific decorator in packages/storybook-addon-codesandbox/src/preview/decorator.tsx renders a CodeSandboxButton (from packages/storybook-addon-codesandbox/src/preview/CodeSandboxButton.tsx), while a framework-agnostic decorator in packages/storybook-addon-codesandbox/src/preview/decorator-dom.ts dynamically creates and appends an HTML button to the DOM. Both approaches provide visual feedback to the user on the export status and ensure that the correct story context is passed for sandbox generation. The CodeSandboxParameters type, defined in packages/storybook-addon-codesandbox/src/types.ts, governs the configuration of these sandbox exports, allowing for specifications like custom files, dependencies, and the target sandbox provider.

Documentation with Interactive Code Examples
The documentation for dnd-kit integrates interactive code examples through Sandpack components from CodeSandbox, enabling users to experiment with code directly within the documentation. This functionality is managed by the apps/docs/sandpack.js file, which dynamically embeds these interactive examples. It achieves this by injecting an importmap script into the document head to define aliases for libraries like React and @dnd-kit packages, ensuring dependencies are correctly resolved within the Sandpack environment.

A custom HTML element, whose behavior is defined by the SandpackElement class in apps/docs/sandpack.js, renders the Sandpack component. This element processes attributes such as height, showTabs, template (e.g., "react", "solid"), and files (a JSON string of file contents) to configure the interactive example. It dynamically builds the necessary dependencies, including @dnd-kit/helpers and framework-specific packages like @dnd-kit/react, based on the specified template. For templates like "solid", it also injects specific infrastructure files. The visual styling of these embedded examples is controlled by a predefined theme object and further customized by apps/docs/style.css, which provides global styling overrides and custom components to maintain a consistent look and feel with the rest of the documentation. This custom styling includes provisions for dark mode, animations for hero sections, and adjustments for various layout elements, ensuring a cohesive user experience across the documentation site. For information on contributing to the documentation, see apps/docs/README.md.
